title: Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita, 2020

The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) and GDP per capita for countries in 2020. The vertical axis measures the AHDI score from 0 to 0.8, while the horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars at 2011 prices on a logarithmic scale ranging from $1,000 to $100,000. Circles sized by population represent each country, with colors distinguishing regions: North America (orange), South America (maroon), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). The data shows a positive relationship where higher income levels coincide with higher development scores. Countries in the lower-left quadrant, including Burundi, DR Congo, Chad, Yemen, and Malawi, cluster with GDP per capita below $2,000 and AHDI scores below 0.3. Moving toward the center, nations like India, Pakistan, and Indonesia sit between $5,000 and $10,000 in income with AHDI scores around 0.4 to 0.5. In the upper-right section, high-income economies such as Switzerland, the United States, Japan, and France display AHDI scores exceeding 0.7 alongside GDP per capita figures above $40,000. Qatar appears as an outlier on the far right with GDP per capita near $100,000 but an AHDI score lower than the top European performers. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time. The overall distribution forms a curve that rises steeply at lower income levels and flattens as GDP per capita increases.
